Invisible push.<break time=800ms/> No contact needed.<break time=800ms/>

The word magnet comes from the Greek magnētis lithos, "stone from Magnesia"—a region where lodestones, naturally magnetic rocks, were first found.<break time=800ms/> These stones could pull iron toward them without touching it, as if defying the rule that forces need contact.<break time=800ms/>

Here’s how it works:<break time=200ms/> Every magnet has two poles, north and south, surrounded by an invisible field of influence.<break time=800ms/> This field isn’t air or energy you can see—it’s a distortion in space itself, created by aligned electrons spinning like tiny tops inside the magnet.<break time=800ms/> When another magnetic material (like iron) enters this field, its own electrons realign, creating an opposing distortion.<break time=800ms/> North repels north;<break time=200ms/> south attracts north.<break time=800ms/> No hands, no strings—just the field doing the work.<break time=800ms/>

Example:<break time=200ms/> A fridge magnet clings because its south pole locks onto the fridge door’s north-polarized metal.<break time=800ms/> Flip it, and the same poles push apart.<break time=800ms/> Stronger magnets, like neodymium ones, can lift 1,000 times their weight because their electron spins are perfectly synchronized, amplifying the field.<break time=800ms/>

Three related subjects are earth’s magnetic core, electromagnetic trains, and how birds navigate fields.<break time=800ms/>
clean_story:
Invisible push. No contact needed.

The word magnet comes from the Greek magnētis lithos, "stone from Magnesia"—a region where lodestones, naturally magnetic rocks, were first found. These stones could pull iron toward them without touching it, as if defying the rule that forces need contact.

Here’s how it works: Every magnet has two poles, north and south, surrounded by an invisible field of influence. This field isn’t air or energy you can see—it’s a distortion in space itself, created by aligned electrons spinning like tiny tops inside the magnet. When another magnetic material (like iron) enters this field, its own electrons realign, creating an opposing distortion. North repels north; south attracts north. No hands, no strings—just the field doing the work.

Example: A fridge magnet clings because its south pole locks onto the fridge door’s north-polarized metal. Flip it, and the same poles push apart. Stronger magnets, like neodymium ones, can lift 1,000 times their weight because their electron spins are perfectly synchronized, amplifying the field.

Three related subjects are earth’s magnetic core, electromagnetic trains, and how birds navigate fields.
